title: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
objectives: Recognize clinical presentations of acute decompensated heart failure
Distinguish HFrEF from HFpEF and understand pathophysiologic differences
Apply appropriate diagnostic workup including BNP and echocardiography
Select evidence-based pharmacotherapy for acute and chronic HF
Identify precipitating factors and address reversible causes

vignette.text:
A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with progressive shortness of breath over 3 days. He reports worsening dyspnea on exertion, now occurring with minimal activity, and has been sleeping upright in a recliner for the past 2 nights due to difficulty breathing when lying flat. He also notes bilateral leg swelling and a 10-pound weight gain over the past week.

Past Medical History: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30% on echo 6 months ago), Coronary artery disease (MI 3 years ago, stents to LAD), Hypertension, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, CKD stage 3 (baseline Cr 1.6)

Medications: Lisinopril 20mg daily, Carvedilol 25mg BID, Furosemide 40mg daily (admits to missing doses for past week due to running out), Atorvastatin 80mg daily, Metformin 1000mg BID, Aspirin 81mg daily

Social History: Former smoker (quit 3 years ago after MI), 2-3 beers on weekends, retired electrician

Allergies: None

physicalExam.general: Elderly man in moderate respiratory distress, sitting upright, speaking in short sentences
physicalExam.cardiovascular: JVP elevated to angle of jaw (~15 cm), S3 gallop present, regular rhythm, displaced PMI, 2/6 holosystolic murmur at apex
physicalExam.pulmonary: Bibasilar crackles to mid-lung fields bilaterally, dullness to percussion at bases
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, mildly distended, hepatomegaly with tender liver edge 4 cm below costal margin, positive hepatojugular reflux
physicalExam.extremities: 3+ pitting edema to knees bilaterally, cool but not mottled

imaging.CXR: Cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusions (R>L), cephalization of pulmonary vessels, Kerley B lines, perihilar haziness (bat-wing pattern)
imaging.Echocardiogram: LVEF 25% (previously 30%), global hypokinesis, moderate mitral regurgitation (functional), RVSP 55 mmHg (elevated), dilated LV (LVEDD 6.8 cm)

ecg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm, left axis deviation, Q waves in V1-V3 (old anterior MI), low voltage in limb leads, no acute ST changes
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia, old anterior MI, no acute ischemia

questions.stem: The patient has an S3 gallop on auscultation.
questions.question: What causes an S3 heart sound and what does it indicate?
questions.hint: Think about rapid ventricular filling in early diastole.

options.text: Atrial contraction against stiff ventricle - indicates hypertrophy
Rapid ventricular filling into a dilated, compliant ventricle - indicates volume overload/HF

questions.explanation: S3 occurs in early diastole when blood rapidly fills a dilated ventricle, causing the ventricular walls to suddenly tense. In adults, S3 indicates pathologic volume overload (HF). S4 is atrial kick against a stiff ventricle (diastolic dysfunction).

whyChain.answer: S3 is best heard at the apex with the bell of the stethoscope, with the patient in the left lateral decubitus position. It's a low-frequency sound.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Rapid ventricular filling into a dilated, compliant ventricle - indicates volume overload/HF.

questions.stem: He has a holosystolic murmur at the apex.
questions.question: What valvular abnormality causes a holosystolic murmur at the apex in heart failure?
questions.hint: Consider what happens when the LV dilates.

options.text: Functional mitral regurgitation from LV dilation and annular stretching

questions.explanation: In dilated cardiomyopathy, the mitral annulus stretches and papillary muscles are displaced, causing incomplete leaflet coaptation and functional (secondary) MR. The murmur is holosystolic, loudest at apex, radiating to axilla.

whyChain.why: Does treating HF improve functional MR?
whyChain.answer: Yes - as the LV remodels with GDMT (guideline-directed medical therapy), the annulus can shrink and MR improves. Severe MR may require surgical/interventional repair (MitraClip).

questions.stem: JVP is elevated to the angle of the jaw.
questions.question: What does elevated JVP indicate in heart failure?
questions.hint: JVP reflects right atrial pressure.

options.text: Elevated right atrial pressure from volume overload and/or RV dysfunction

questions.explanation: JVP directly reflects right atrial pressure. In left HF, elevated LA pressure transmits backward through pulmonary circulation to RV, then RA. JVP >8 cm suggests elevated filling pressures. His JVP at jaw angle (~15 cm) indicates severe congestion.

whyChain.answer: Sustained JVP elevation (>3 cm for >15 sec) with abdominal pressure indicates the RV cannot accommodate increased venous return - sensitive sign of elevated filling pressures even when resting JVP is normal.

questions.stem: The chest X-ray shows cephalization of pulmonary vessels.
questions.question: What causes cephalization (redistribution) of pulmonary blood flow?
questions.hint: Consider the effect of elevated LA pressure on pulmonary vasculature.

options.text: Elevated LA pressure causes lower lobe vessel constriction, redirecting flow to upper lobes

questions.explanation: Normally, gravity causes more blood flow to lung bases. Elevated LA pressure (>12-15 mmHg) causes interstitial edema and reflex vasoconstriction in dependent areas, redistributing flow to upper lobes (cephalization) - an early sign of pulmonary congestion.

questions.stem: His ejection fraction is 25%.
questions.question: What determines cardiac output, and how is it affected in HFrEF?
questions.hint: CO = HR × SV. Consider what affects stroke volume.

options.text: CO = HR × SV; in HFrEF, reduced contractility decreases SV despite compensatory increased HR
CO depends only on heart rate
CO is fixed and cannot change

questions.explanation: Cardiac output = Heart rate × Stroke volume. Stroke volume depends on preload, afterload, and contractility. In HFrEF, impaired contractility reduces SV. The heart compensates with increased HR and preload (Frank-Starling), but eventually decompensates.

whyChain.answer: Increased preload (ventricular filling) stretches myocytes, increasing sarcomere overlap and contractile force. In HF, the curve is flattened - increased preload yields less SV improvement, and eventually causes pulmonary congestion.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! CO = HR × SV; in HFrEF, reduced contractility decreases SV despite compensatory increased HR.

questions.stem: His BNP is 1,840 pg/mL.
questions.question: What triggers BNP release and what is its physiologic function?
questions.hint: BNP comes from the ventricles in response to stretch.

options.text: Ventricular wall stretch releases BNP, which causes natriuresis, vasodilation, and antagonizes RAAS
Kidney releases BNP in response to low sodium
Atrial stretch releases BNP, which causes vasoconstriction
Liver releases BNP in response to congestion

questions.explanation: BNP (B-type natriuretic peptide) is released from ventricular myocytes in response to wall stretch/pressure overload. It promotes natriuresis and diuresis, vasodilation, and inhibits RAAS - a compensatory counter-regulatory response to volume overload.

whyChain.why: What BNP level rules out HF?
whyChain.answer: BNP <100 pg/mL (or NT-proBNP <300) has high negative predictive value for ruling out acute HF. BNP >400 strongly suggests HF. Gray zone 100-400 requires clinical correlation. Obesity lowers BNP; renal failure raises it.

questions.stem: His sodium is 132 mEq/L (low).
questions.question: Why is hyponatremia common in heart failure?
questions.hint: Consider neurohormonal activation and water handling.

options.text: Elevated ADH (from low effective circulating volume) causes water retention diluting sodium

questions.explanation: In HF, low cardiac output triggers neurohormonal activation including ADH release (non-osmotic stimulus from perceived low volume). ADH causes water retention, diluting serum sodium. Hyponatremia in HF indicates severe neurohormonal activation and poor prognosis.

whyChain.why: How do you treat hyponatremia in HF?
whyChain.answer: Fluid restriction (1-1.5 L/day) and treating underlying HF. Severe cases may require vasopressin antagonists (tolvaptan). Don't give hypertonic saline - these patients are volume overloaded, not depleted.

questions.stem: His creatinine rose from baseline 1.6 to 2.1 mg/dL.
questions.question: What causes worsening renal function in acute decompensated HF?
questions.hint: Consider both forward and backward failure.

options.text: Direct cardiac toxin effect on kidneys
Both reduced renal perfusion (low CO) and venous congestion (elevated CVP) impair GFR

questions.explanation: Cardiorenal syndrome: (1) Low CO reduces renal perfusion; (2) Venous congestion elevates renal venous pressure, reducing GFR. Congestion may be more important than low CO. Decongestion often improves renal function despite initial creatinine rise.

whyChain.why: Should you hold diuretics if creatinine rises?
whyChain.answer: Not necessarily. If patient is still congested, continue diuresis - renal function often improves as congestion clears. Worsening renal function from decongestion often doesn't worsen outcomes. Persistent congestion is more harmful.

questions.stem: He has HFrEF with EF 25%.
questions.question: What is the key pathophysiologic difference between HFrEF and HFpEF?
questions.hint: Consider systolic vs diastolic function.

options.text: HFrEF is acute; HFpEF is chronic
HFrEF: impaired contractility (systolic dysfunction); HFpEF: impaired relaxation/filling (diastolic dysfunction)
HFrEF is right heart failure; HFpEF is left heart failure

questions.explanation: HFrEF (EF ≤40%): primary problem is impaired contraction - the heart can't pump effectively. HFpEF (EF ≥50%): primary problem is impaired relaxation/compliance - the heart can't fill adequately. Both cause congestion and low output symptoms.

whyChain.answer: GDMT (ACEi, BB, MRA, ARNI, SGLT2i) dramatically reduces mortality in HFrEF. In HFpEF, only SGLT2 inhibitors have shown mortality benefit - other therapies treat symptoms but don't improve survival.

questions.stem: His prior MI caused his cardiomyopathy.
questions.question: How does myocardial infarction lead to heart failure?
questions.hint: Consider what happens to dead heart muscle.

options.text: MI does not cause heart failure
MI causes immediate heart failure in all cases
MI causes myocyte death → scar formation → loss of contractile tissue → remodeling → dilated LV

questions.explanation: After MI, infarcted tissue becomes non-contractile scar. Remaining myocardium compensates with hypertrophy and dilation. Over time, adverse remodeling leads to progressive LV dilation and dysfunction - ischemic cardiomyopathy.

whyChain.answer: Yes - early revascularization limits infarct size. Post-MI, ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, and MRAs prevent adverse remodeling. This is why these drugs are started in all post-MI patients regardless of initial EF.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! MI causes myocyte death → scar formation → loss of contractile tissue → remodeling → dilated LV.

questions.stem: He stopped his diuretic for the past week.
questions.question: What is the most common precipitant of acute decompensated heart failure?
questions.hint: Consider medication and dietary factors.

options.text: Medication/dietary non-adherence (stopping diuretics, high sodium intake)
New myocardial infarction in all cases

questions.explanation: Non-adherence is the #1 precipitant of ADHF (40-50% of cases). Other precipitants: ischemia/MI, arrhythmias (especially new AFib), infection, anemia, uncontrolled HTN, renal dysfunction, drugs (NSAIDs, negative inotropes).

whyChain.answer: ECG (ischemia, arrhythmia), troponin (demand ischemia common), BMP (renal function, electrolytes), CBC (anemia, infection), TSH (if new onset), and detailed med/dietary history.

questions.hint: Consider supply-demand mismatch.

options.text: Myocardial oxygen supply-demand mismatch (type 2 MI) from tachycardia and wall stress

questions.stem: Acute management requires diuresis for decongestion.
questions.question: What is the initial diuretic strategy for acute decompensated HF?
questions.hint: Consider the patient's home diuretic dose.

options.text: IV furosemide at 1-2.5× home oral dose, given IV for better bioavailability
Oral furosemide at same home dose

questions.explanation: Give IV loop diuretic (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide) at dose equal to or 2.5× the home oral dose. IV has better bioavailability in gut edema. He takes furosemide 40mg PO daily, so start 40-100mg IV. Reassess and escalate if inadequate response.

whyChain.why: What if he doesn't respond to IV furosemide?
whyChain.answer: Diuretic resistance strategies: (1) Increase dose; (2) Continuous infusion; (3) Add thiazide (metolazone) for sequential nephron blockade; (4) Consider ultrafiltration if refractory.

questions.stem: He is on lisinopril and carvedilol.
questions.question: What are the four pillars of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF?
questions.hint: Recent guidelines added a fourth pillar.

options.text: ARNI (or ACEi/ARB), Beta-blocker, MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone), SGLT2 inhibitor

questions.explanation: The four pillars: (1) ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) preferred over ACEi/ARB; (2) Beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol); (3) MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone); (4) SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). All four improve survival.

whyChain.why: Should GDMT be started during acute decompensation?
whyChain.answer: Continue home BB and ACEi/ARNI if BP tolerates - don't stop. New initiation usually waits until euvolemic and stable. SGLT2i can be started before discharge. Rapid up-titration post-discharge improves outcomes.

questions.stem: His BP is 152/94 despite being on lisinopril.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of benefit of ACE inhibitors/ARBs/ARNI in HFrEF?
questions.hint: Consider neurohormonal modulation.

options.text: Block harmful RAAS activation, reduce afterload, prevent adverse remodeling

questions.explanation: ACEi/ARB/ARNI block the harmful effects of chronic RAAS activation in HF: vasoconstriction, sodium retention, myocyte hypertrophy, fibrosis, and adverse remodeling. ARNI adds neprilysin inhibition, boosting beneficial natriuretic peptides.

whyChain.why: Why is ARNI preferred over ACEi?
whyChain.answer: PARADIGM-HF showed sacubitril/valsartan reduced mortality 20% vs enalapril. The neprilysin inhibition enhances beneficial natriuretic peptides while ARB blocks harmful RAAS. Switch from ACEi if tolerating and EF ≤40%.

questions.stem: He is on carvedilol 25mg BID.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of beta-blocker benefit in HFrEF?
questions.hint: Consider chronic sympathetic activation in HF.

options.text: Block harmful chronic sympathetic activation, reduce HR, prevent remodeling

questions.explanation: Chronic sympathetic activation in HF causes tachycardia, arrhythmias, myocyte toxicity, and adverse remodeling. Beta-blockers block these effects. They may initially worsen symptoms (negative inotropy) but long-term improve EF and reduce mortality by 30-35%.

whyChain.why: Should you hold beta-blocker during acute decompensation?
whyChain.answer: Generally continue unless severely hypotensive or in cardiogenic shock. Stopping abruptly can cause rebound sympathetic surge. May need to reduce dose if hypotensive. Don't initiate new BB during acute episode.

questions.stem: His potassium is 4.8 mEq/L and creatinine 2.1 mg/dL.
questions.question: What monitoring is required when adding an MRA (spironolactone)?
questions.hint: Consider electrolyte effects and renal function.

options.text: Monitor potassium and creatinine - avoid if K+ >5.0 or severe renal dysfunction

questions.explanation: MRAs cause potassium retention and can worsen renal function. Check K+ and Cr within 1 week of starting and periodically. Avoid if K+ >5.0 or eGFR <30 (spironolactone) or <25 (eplerenone). Reduce dose if K+ 5.0-5.5.

whyChain.why: What's the mortality benefit of MRAs?
whyChain.answer: RALES showed 30% mortality reduction with spironolactone in severe HF. EMPHASIS-HF showed eplerenone benefit in mild-moderate HF. MRAs block harmful aldosterone effects: fibrosis, sodium retention, potassium wasting.

questions.stem: SGLT2 inhibitors are now part of GDMT.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of SGLT2 inhibitor benefit in HFrEF?
questions.hint: Effects extend beyond glucose lowering.

options.text: Glucose lowering is the only mechanism
Glucosuria causes osmotic diuresis/natriuresis, plus direct cardiac benefits independent of glucose

questions.explanation: SGLT2i cause glucosuria leading to osmotic diuresis and natriuresis. But benefits in HF occur even without diabetes, suggesting direct cardiac effects: improved energetics, reduced fibrosis, decreased preload/afterload. DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced showed mortality benefit.

whyChain.why: Can you use SGLT2i in patients without diabetes?
whyChain.answer: Yes! DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced included non-diabetic patients with HFrEF - benefits were similar regardless of diabetes status. indicated for HFrEF regardless of diabetes.

options.text: Warm and Dry - adequate perfusion, euvolemic

questions.stem: Telemetry shows a new wide-complex tachycardia at 180 bpm.
questions.question: What arrhythmia complication is common in HFrEF?
questions.hint: Scar and remodeling create arrhythmia substrate.

options.text: Ventricular tachycardia - common cause of sudden cardiac death in HF

questions.explanation: VT is common in HFrEF due to scar from prior MI, creating re-entrant circuits. Sudden cardiac death accounts for ~50% of HF mortality. This is why ICDs are indicated for primary prevention in HFrEF with EF ≤35% despite optimal GDMT.

whyChain.answer: With EF 25% and ischemic cardiomyopathy, he likely qualifies for primary prevention ICD if EF remains ≤35% after 3+ months of optimal GDMT. Guidelines require waiting 40 days post-MI and 90 days post-revascularization.

questions.stem: Despite maximum diuretics, he remains severely congested.
questions.question: What option exists for diuretic-refractory fluid overload?
questions.hint: Consider mechanical fluid removal.

options.text: Ultrafiltration - mechanical removal of plasma water and sodium
IV fluids to improve kidney function
Discharge and follow up as outpatient

questions.explanation: Ultrafiltration removes isotonic fluid (water + sodium) directly. Used for diuretic-refractory congestion. CARRESS-HF showed no superiority over stepped diuretics, so reserve for true diuretic failure. Aquapheresis is similar technology.

mcqs.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.explanation: ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.
LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.
Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.
LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
